item.title: love it. excellent deconstruction of the "Sealed Evil In A Can" trope
item.content:encoded: reminds me of 2317-J an d Clef's own comment on 2317's discussion thread.

item.title: Re: first +400
item.content:encoded: congratulations!!

item.title: first +400
item.content:encoded: pog

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: oh i love this. +1. i knew there would be something screwy going on, especially with that overdramatic header, but the ending caught me by surprise in a good way. great setup, great payoff, and just long enough.
HOLY FUCK

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Let's hope the same thing happened to SCP-2317!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh, that was… That was interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly my first thought on reading was that 3 cadavers and the heart of an angel are simply not enough food to sustain the metabolism of a 40m tall creature, so it starved to death, and the Messages From Beyond warning humanity to reseal the lock were something automatic built into the initial containment by the ancient wizards.

I guess a fourth possible interpretation is that the Child wasn't being contained at all, but was murdered and then sealed in, with reams of fabricated "ancient lore" about how terrible it was and how important it is to keep contained then dispersed throughout various libraries of the ancient world to keep the evidence of the crime hidden, and modern humans were the only ones incompetent enough to mess up the ritual and therefore discover the body. That is probably a more serious implication than intended by the author though.

item.title: Re: Keter to Neutralized
item.content:encoded: Doing so would ruin the ending twist and kill all the tension building up to that moment, so no.

item.title: Keter to Neutralized
item.content:encoded: Guys, according to the SCP-4290 addendum 4290.4 the object had been neutralized. So can I edit the Objective class to "strikethrough" keter to neutralized and a little change on special containment procedure?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent stuff! Couple of things that seemed a little out of place:

1) Where was the energy to break the seals coming from? Probably doesn't need explanation, but it might be something to mention in the UNLOCK addendum as coming from the seal breakdown itself. The Foundation seems like they would be thorough in studying what happened.
2) The weirdly specific warning system, limited to television? If you're building an anomalous warning system 10,000 years ago, you'd think it would just aim at everything around with signal capability.

Both kind of make it feel a little less like a standard SCP, but great piece!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I found this independently of jamcon, and it was great.

item.title: Re: +200 \o/
item.content:encoded: What can I say, it's well-written!

item.title: +200 \o/
item.content:encoded: Holy shit that was fast!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a small typo that I didn't notice until now. Just fixed it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I absolutely love the story, payoff is brilliant.

Question: Is the final addendum supposed to be 4290.3 again? Or should it be 4290.4?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: very good

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's amazing and I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had no idea what the theme of the contest was.

This did knock my socks off, indeed… Probably because if you were fed human corpses for ten thousand years, this is exactly what would happen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'll pretend that the cleverness of the first interpretation was intentional. Or that the second was something I was thinking about as I was writing. Glad you enjoyed reading!

On a sidenote, this SCP is now my highest rated article on the wiki! (2fast2quick)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeap, this is a +1 earned. I knew this was for JamCon, but since I treat JamCon like the Grinch (I wouldn't touch it with a thirty-nine and a half foot pole) I wasn't really expecting the ending. Well, I had an inkling, right near the end. Once I saw that there was something written, at all, after it woke up, I was sure something strange and unexpected had happened. Anyways, this was good.

There are two interpretations here, and I like both. One is that this thing was a literal child, and was so fussy about being sealed that it threw a fit and refused to eat its sacrifices… or, two, the gods we think we need to appease only have power because of us, and it is our belief in their powers that makes us fear, not their actual powers. In essence, if you and everyone else suddenly start neglecting a god, it's not like they can damn you, and that's what happened to this thing.

Good article, and highly deserving of winning JamCon (if anything is). Good job, and +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Stumbled here without knowing about the contest. Can confirm that I was caught off guard by the ending.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Didn't know the contest theme and this was great. Nice work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The cause of death was determined to be malnutrition.

AHAHAHAHA

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great escalation and foreshadowing. That ending totally caught me by surprise. I added this to my "to read" list when it appeared in recently created, but didn't end up read it until now, after I had forgotten this was a jamcon entry. Goddamn it lol, that got me.

+1
holy fuck that was fast

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like the wiki already passed the amount of nigh unstoppable, immortal, world destroying eldritch abominations that there can be before they get tiring and lose their impact. Which is why I absolutely love the way this ended.

+1 would forget to feed my pet TamagoChild.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Loved the initial concept, and loved the ending. The middle flags a bit, only because of the constraints of the passive voice on such a narrative-heavy skip. I could see some quotes or report transcripts added to break that up a bit, but it's already a good skip without them.

I'm not sure what is meant, though, that the TV broadcast "repeated in frequency."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have been relatively inactive recently and was unaware of the contest, and I can confirm that my socks have been knocked off.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
and I can only imagine it'll be even better when people come back to it after the contest is over with.

Going through Recently Created, I had no idea this was a contest entry. I can confirm that it's an absolute riot without that expectation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the best take I've seen on a classic "shaggy dog story" so far. It's good right now, and I can only imagine it'll be even better when people come back to it after the contest is over with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. However, this is still excellent, and will be knocking people's socks off months from now once jamcon 2019 is forgotten about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a really neat idea, but I feel that knowing the theme of the contest spoils the twist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved in draft, love it now. This is very very good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If I hadn’t pitched an idea exactly like this into chat several months ago, that ending would have thrown me for a complete loop. A fantastic interpretation of the theme.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"In its original sense, a shaggy dog story or yarn is an extremely long-winded anecdote characterized by extensive narration of typically irrelevant incidents and terminated by an anticlimax or a pointless punchline."

Originally I wasn't going to be participating in Jamcon 2 (what with INTcon and other projects) but then I figured I may as well participate. So, here's my Day 1 entry!

Thanks to MaliceAforethought, Weryllium, Uncle Nicolini, and Mortos for crit!

Image credit:

  • https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Qinghai_lake.jpg
